chunk_summary.idea_trajectory_justification: The group spent the segment reviewing their shared document, clarifying terminology, and finalizing four specific bullet points to represent their shared project idea.
chunk_summary.collective_engagement_justification: Non-speaking participants displayed consistent responsiveness, including visible head nods, smiles, and active engagement with the shared document.
chunk_summary.cross_disciplinary_bridging_description: Bridged physiological metabolic processes with broader spatial and ecological scales.
chunk_summary.artifact_interaction_description: Participants actively edited the document, moved text to speaker notes, and corrected terminology.
chunk_summary.problem_specificity_justification: The group articulated a specific approach: predicting spillover at intermediate scales using metabolic and immunological markers combined with AI.
chunk_summary.decision_crystallization_justification: The group explicitly agreed upon four main bullet points that represent their synthesized project direction.
chunk_summary.shared_vision_quote: make sure we have agreement for the team
chunk_summary.funding_reference_description: Linda wished the participants good luck on their proposals.
chunk_summary.notable_observation: The group successfully synthesized their divergent ideas into four concrete bullet points on the shared document, actively clarifying terminology (e.g., 'bridging' vs 'breaching' scales) and ensuring consensus before the session ended. The facilitator also took time at the end to check in on the participants' experience with the virtual format, fostering a supportive relational climate.

ideas_currently_on_table: Automated risk assessment pipeline using wildlife data
Connecting viral evolution scales with ecological scales
Incorporating human behavior into spillover models
Integrating eco-immunology and metabolism into predictive models
Predicting spillover at intermediate spatial scales
chunk_summaries: The group spent the segment conducting structured round-robin introductions, with each participant sharing their disciplinary background and current research focus. The facilitator, Linda, guided the process and coordinated access to a shared Google document for note-taking. The tone was polite and professional, ending with the group preparing to transition into brainstorming ideas.

Kim initiated the scientific brainstorming by posing a question about the relative importance of predicting spillover events versus establishing surveillance systems. This sparked a divergent discussion, with Kezia challenging the mutual exclusivity of the two approaches and Daniel providing a concrete example of fine-scale ecological prediction using the Hendra virus system. The group shifted from individual introductions to joint problem framing, demonstrating active engagement through visible nods and collaborative dialogue.

The group engaged in a divergent exploration of the drivers of spillover events, debating the relative importance of ecological contact versus viral evolution. Kim Pepin highlighted a critical gap in connecting evolutionary and ecological scales, which Daniel Becker supported with findings from his machine learning research. The tone remained collaborative and intellectually engaged, with participants actively building on each other's points, though no specific project direction was finalized.

The group engaged in a highly divergent and generative brainstorming session, sequentially proposing new variables to integrate into predictive models of spillover. Adela suggested incorporating human behavior, Kezia proposed combining dynamic systems with machine learning, Kim introduced eco-immunology, Daniel expanded on within-host traits, and Adela added metabolic data. The tone was enthusiastic and collaborative, with participants explicitly building on each other's ideas and demonstrating strong engagement through visible nods and shared smiles. The chunk ended with Linda attempting to ground these theoretical expansions in a real-world scenario using influenza viruses.

The group continued their divergent exploration, with Linda and Troy discussing the role of human behavior in avian influenza spillover. Mike introduced a cross-disciplinary analogy, comparing disease emergence prediction to forecasting earthquakes, suggesting the problem be broken down by scale. Kezia, Kim, and Adela then deeply expanded on the idea of using metabolic markers to identify spillover hotspots and super-spreaders, while Matt introduced the potential of microbiomes. The tone remained highly collaborative and intellectually engaged, with participants actively building on each other's specific variables, though no single project direction was finalized.

The group successfully synthesized their divergent brainstorming into a cohesive framework, focusing on predicting spillover at 'intermediate spatial scales' by linking metabolic and immunological markers with environmental covariates. Kezia and Daniel drove this integration, bridging high-resolution movement ecology with broad-scale predictions. In the final minutes, Mike reminded the group of the time, prompting Linda and Adela to share a Google Slide and transition the team into a procedural phase to summarize their main points for the upcoming report-out. The chunk ended with the group actively reviewing their documented ideas, demonstrating strong consensus and shared vision.

The group spent the final segment converging on their shared ideas, successfully synthesizing their brainstorming into four concrete bullet points on the shared document. Adela and Kezia clarified their respective interpretations of 'intermediate scales', leading to a synthesized view that bridged physiological metabolic processes with broader spatial and ecological scales. The tone was highly collaborative and appreciative, with participants actively editing the document and ensuring consensus. The session concluded with the facilitator checking in on the participants' virtual conference experience, ending on a positive and reflective note.
